channel.title: Los Angeles Review of Books – Reviews
channel.description: Book reviews from the Los Angeles Review of Books.

item.title: Murky Effects
item.description: A novel of desire and domestic disorder amid a disintegrating literary establishment.

item.title: Red-Pilling Speculation
item.description: From Ayn Rand to QAnon, a new anthology explores how reactionary world-building becomes reactionary world-making.

item.title: Dancing in Shackles
item.description: Two new books about internet control in China and North Korea address the potent question of what it means to be free online.

item.title: The Deadline Dickens
item.description: A new biography of Garry Trudeau offers a wealth of detail but fails to come to grips with the limits of his comic strip’s satire.

item.title: An Epic of Displacement
item.description: Boris Dralyuk’s latest work of translation is Alexander Voloshin’s poetic epic about a Ukrainian exile lost in Hollywood.

item.title: California Sons: Miriam Pawel’s “The Browns of California”
item.description: Jake Wertz reviews “The Browns of California,” a four-generation biography of the Brown family and a portrait of the state they shaped.

item.title: Poem on Fire
item.description: Larry Levis’s collected poems tell the present moment to go to hell.

item.title: Traces of Looking and Feeling
item.description: A George Whitmore reissue translates the historical feeling of queer coming-of-age in the 1950s for contemporary readers without slipping into exploitation.

item.title: The University as Giant App
item.description: Theo Baker’s book about Stanford offers a shockingly frank look at a campus that is as tightly governed as a Siberian labor camp—one perhaps designed by Sergey Brin.

item.title: What the ‘Footprint’ Forgets
item.description: Radhika Subramaniam unsettles the Anthropocene’s dominant metaphor for humans’ impact on the earth.

item.title: Trauma Is a Narrative Problem
item.description: In Emily LaBarge’s new memoir, trauma’s intensity and unknowable nature impel her to try to communicate it.

item.title: Exquisite Objects, Exquisite Creature
item.description: On Barbara Pym’s reissued novel of desire, aging, possession, and antiques.

item.title: Glowing and Mundane
item.description: Exploring an exhibition of paintings by Fred Reichman, a Bay Area bohemian who blended modernism and Zen in works of calligraphic spareness.

item.title: Everybody Wants This
item.description: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ wants to rehabilitate the fashion industry’s brutal early 2000s workplace culture, but it can’t quite hide how much it misses it.
